title: Interplay between Aharonov-Bohm and Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak oscillations in phase-pure GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires of different lengths
authors: Farah Basarić, Kaiwen Wang, Tudor-Gabriel Dumitru, Andrei Manolescu, Francisco Alvarado Cesar, Ana M. Sanchez, Christoph Krause, Detlev Grützmacher, Alexander Pawlis, Thomas Schäpers
abstract: In GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires, comprising a tubular conducting shell, interference phenomena observed under an axial field and originating from closed-loop states encircling the insulating core, provide an ideal platform for superconducting quantum devices that utilize effects such as Aharonov--Bohm or Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak-type conductance oscillations. Both effects are different in nature with respect to phase rigidity because of interference of non-time-reversed or time-reversed paths, respectively. Since their occurrence is largely governed by averaging effects, which depend on sample dimensions and the transport regime, we present a systematic study of flux-periodic oscillations of phase-pure zinc-blende GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires as a function of gate voltage for samples with different contact separation lengths. Our analysis shows that with increasing contact separation length, averaging effects result in gradual reduction of $h/e$-periodic Aharonov--Bohm-type oscillations, while the $h/2e$-periodic Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak oscillations and its $h/4e$-periodic higher harmonics are enhanced. The additional phase rigidity seen in the $h/3e$-periodic oscillations is attributed to phase rigidity propagating from the neighbouring lower harmonics. Our tight-binding transport simulations on nanowires of different lengths which contain only a few scattering centers confirm the experimental observations regarding the different harmonics and their phase rigidity. Together, our experimental and simulation findings indicate quasi-ballistic transport with persistent Aharonov--Bohm-, and phase-rigid Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak-type oscillations despite few scattering centers.
summary: The study investigates how Aharonov-Bohm and Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak oscillations manifest in GaAs/InAs nanowires as a function of wire length. By analyzing magnetoconductance, the authors show that longer wires enhance the phase-rigid AAS signal relative to the AB signal. This work confirms quasi-ballistic transport regimes suitable for quantum device applications.
main_problem: The study investigates the interplay between Aharonov-Bohm (AB) and Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak (AAS) conductance oscillations in GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires as a function of contact separation length.
main_result: Increasing the contact separation length enhances phase-rigid AAS oscillations ($h/2e$-periodic) while causing the AB oscillations ($h/e$-periodic) to diminish due to averaging effects. The presence of higher harmonics ($h/3e, h/4e$) is also observed and analyzed.
method: The research combines experimental magnetotransport measurements (measuring conductance vs. magnetic field and gate voltage) with theoretical analysis using tight-binding quantum transport simulations (Landauer–Büttiker formalism).
model_or_system: The physical system is phase-pure GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires, which act as tubular conductors exhibiting interference phenomena from closed loops encircling an insulating core when subjected to an axial magnetic field.
key_observables: Magnetoconductance oscillations periodic in $h/e$ (AB) and $h/2e$ (AAS), phase rigidity of these oscillations, and the amplitude dependence on sample length ($L_c$).
important_parameters: Contact separation length ($L_c$), magnetic field ($B$), gate voltage ($V_g$), and harmonic indices ($n=1, 2, 3, 4$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed oscillations can be separated into distinct harmonic components related to AB (non-time-reversed paths) and AAS (time-reversed paths) interference.
figures_summary: Figures show TEM/SEM images of nanowires with varying contact separations, color maps and averaged magnetoconductance plots revealing periodicities ($h/e$ vs $h/2e$), and FFT spectra confirming the presence and relative strength of higher harmonics up to $h/4e$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes experimental data across samples with different contact lengths, comparing the decay of AB oscillations against the enhancement of AAS oscillations. Theoretical simulations are used to confirm these length-dependent trends and analyze the contribution of specific harmonic terms.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed platform for studying fundamental quantum interference effects (AB vs. AAS) in mesoscopic semiconductor nanostructures, which is highly relevant for designing superconducting quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Low-magnification TEM image of a representative example of the GaAs/InAs nanowire, featuring (b) high- resolution TEM image of the nanowire middle region. (c),(d) False color SEM image of sample C and E, respectively, emphasizing differences in inner contact separation lengths Lc, 600 nm and 2 µm, respectively.

FIG. 2: Normalized magnetoconductance G/G0, with G0 = e2/h, for various gate voltage Vg values, for (a) sample C, and (b) E, respectively. Corresponding fast-Fourier transform of the data shown in (a) and (b), for samples (c) C, and (d) E. Ranges of present harmonics (h/e, h/2e, h/3e, h/4e) are presented in color-coded scheme, with mean frequency value marked by dotted line. Colormaps for oscillation amplitude δG as a function of gate voltage and magnetic field for sample C, presented for (e) h/e-, (f) h/2e-, (g) h/3e-, and (h) h/4e-periodic oscillations defined by the frequency windows marked in (b). Averaged oscillation amplitude ⟨δG⟩Vg over the complete gate voltage range, as a function...

FIG. 3: (a) Normalized average of fast Fourier Ampli- tude ⟨AFFT⟩Vg as a function of frequency for samples with different contact separations (samples A to E). (b) Ratio of the average amplitude of the AAS oscillations ⟨δG⟩h/2e Vg,B=0 at B = 0 and the root-mean-square ampli- tude of the h/e-periodic AB-type oscillations, averaged over the gate voltage ⟨rms(δG)⟩h/2 Vg for different contact separation lengths.

FIG. 4: Panels (a)-(d), (i)-(l), and (q)-(t) show the conductance oscillation amplitudes of first to fourth harmonics, with periods from h/e to h/4e, as a function of the magnetic field and chemical potential, for nanowires of lengths L = 600 nm, 2 µm, and 4 µm, respectively. Panels (e)-(h), (m)-(p), and (u)-(x) display the average amplitude of the oscillation (solid blue line) and its standard deviation (in green) with respect to the chemical potential µ for each length. Note that the displayed magnetic field range is successively reduced for the higher harmonics.

FIG. 5: (a) Normalized fast Fourier amplitude ⟨AFFT⟩µ averaged over µ as a function of frequency f for the 600 nm (light blue), 2 µm (red), and 4 µm (dark blue) long nanowires. (b) Ratio of the average amplitude of the simulated AAS-type oscillations ⟨δG⟩h/2e µ,B=0 at B = 0 and the root-mean-square amplitude of the simulated h/e- periodic AB-type oscillations averaged over the chemical potential ⟨rms(δG)⟩h/2e µ .
